critic_raw: The IT support retrieval experiment uses Recall@10 as the primary metric. The label is 'the article(s) consulted by the support agent during resolution, inferred from explicit selection in the ticketing system or timestamped article access logs.' This label may not reflect the best article for resolution — agents may consult multiple articles including irrelevant ones, or they may know the answer without accessing any article. Additionally, the design uses a temporal split for a knowledge base that may change — articles added in months 1-12 are used for training but articles added in months 13-18 would only be in the validation/test corpus. The dense retrieval model was not trained on queries about articles added after month 12. How does it handle retrieval for new articles?
defender_raw: On label quality: the design includes manual expert review of 200-300 tickets to 'verify label quality and identify cases where multiple articles are legitimately correct.' This audit directly addresses the concern about agent behavior — multi-article consultations are flagged as 'multiple correct articles' and handled in Recall@10 (which naturally accommodates multi-label data). On new articles: both BM25 and the dense retrieval model can retrieve newly added articles — BM25 uses the current article index at test time, while the dense model encodes new articles using the frozen encoder (applied to new article text). The dense encoder was trained on query-article pairs from months 1-12 but generalizes to new articles via the pretrained backbone. This is a valid deployment scenario — the encoder is frozen and generalizes to new documents.
adjudication_raw: Two concerns: label quality from agent behavior and new article retrieval for dense model. Both addressed: expert audit validates label quality; frozen encoder generalizes to new articles. Verdict: defense_wins.
